title: Everyday Situations
description: Food ordering, getting around, and shopping — the three situations you'll face every day in Delhi.
culturalNote: In Delhi, 'bhaiya' (brother) is what you call waiters, auto drivers, shopkeepers, and any unknown man you need help from. 'Didi' (sister) for women. It's respectful and warm — don't be shy about using it.

exercises.note: 'Bhaiya' = respectful address for a waiter, shopkeeper, auto driver, etc. 'Dena' = give (imperative, polite). You can also say 'menu dikhao' (show me the menu).
Code-switched with English 'recommend'. Completely natural. 'Karoge' = will you do (future, informal). Also works: 'kya acha hai yahan?' = what's good here?
'Teekha' = spicy/hot. 'Thoda teekha' = a little spicy. 'Bahut teekha mat karna' = don't make it too spicy. Important to know if you're not used to Indian spice levels!
'Bill' (English word) + 'le aana' = bring (lit: take and come). 'Bhaiya, bill le aana' is the standard way to call for the check. 'Bill' works universally — no need for 'hisaab'.

exercises.prompt: You've finished your meal and want the check. What do you say to the waiter?

exercises.note: 'Ek aur' = one more. 'Lao' = bring (slightly more direct than 'le aana'). 'Ek aur chai chahiye' = I need one more tea. 'Ek aur' works for anything you want another of.

exercises.english: Go straight, then take a right
exercises.spanish: Sigue recto, luego gira a la derecha
exercises.note: 'Seedha' = straight. 'Right lo' = take a right. 'Left lo' = take a left. 'U-turn lo' = take a U-turn. Directions in Delhi often mix Hindi and English freely.
'Kitni door' = how far. 'Bahut door nahi' = not very far. 'Paas hi hai' = it's nearby/close. 'Paidal jaoge toh 10 minute lagenge' = if you walk, it'll take 10 minutes.

exercises.english: Brother, how much will you charge?
exercises.note: Always ask before getting in an auto. 'Loge' = will you take. If the price is too high: 'bahut zyada hai bhaiya' then 'thoda kam karo na'. Meter nahi use karte toh price negotiate karo.

exercises.prompt: You need directions and want to ask 'how far is it?' What do you say?

exercises.english: How long is the metro running? / Until when is the metro?
exercises.note: 'Kab tak' = until when / how long. Also: 'metro kab aayegi?' = when will the metro come? 'Agli metro kab hai?' = when is the next metro? Delhi metro is a lifesaver — know how to ask about it.
'Yeh' = this. The most basic shopping question. Point at the item. 'Woh kitne ka hai?' = how much is that? 'Yeh wala' = this one (pointing). 'Woh wala' = that one.

exercises.english: Show me more / Show more options
exercises.note: 'Dikhao' = show (imperative). 'Aur kuch dikhao' = show me something else. 'Iska aur color hai?' = does this have another color? 'Size chhota hai, bada dikhao' = size is small, show bigger.

exercises.sentence: Yeh too small hai. _____ size mein dikhao.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: This is too small. Show me in a _____ size.
exercises.hint: Bigger — the adjective for 'big' in plural/formal form

exercises.english: Is that for sure? / Are you sure? / Is it guaranteed?
exercises.note: 'Pakka' = sure/fixed/confirmed. Used everywhere: 'kal aana hai, pakka?' = you're definitely coming tomorrow? 'Pakka!' as a standalone = absolutely, for sure! Key Delhi word.

exercises.prompt: You're bargaining for a kurti and the seller gives you a price. You want to start negotiating. What do you say?

exercises.note: 'Khaana' = food. 'Ready' (English) + 'hai' = is ready. Completely natural hybrid. You can also say 'order aa gaya?' = has the order arrived? in restaurants.

exercises.sentence: Bhaiya, _____ hai? Bahut der ho gayi.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: Brother, is _____ ready? It's been very long.
exercises.hint: The thing you're waiting for in a restaurant

exercises.prompt: You're in an auto and want to tell the driver to go straight and then turn left. What do you say?
